title: Paracetamol (Acetaminophen)
content: Paracetamol (also known as Acetaminophen) is an analgesic and antipyretic drug used to treat mild to moderate pain and fever. Common brand names include Dolo, Calpol, and Tylenol. Standard adult dose is 500mg to 1000mg every 4 to 6 hours, not exceeding 4000mg per day. It is available in tablet, syrup, and IV formulations. Overdose can cause severe hepatotoxicity. It is safe in pregnancy and commonly used in children. Contraindicated in patients with severe hepatic impairment.

title: Ibuprofen
content: Ibuprofen is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) used for pain, fever, and inflammation. Standard adult dose is 200mg to 400mg every 4 to 6 hours, maximum 1200mg per day for OTC use and up to 3200mg per day under medical supervision. Common brands include Brufen, Advil, and Nurofen. Side effects include GI irritation, peptic ulcers, renal impairment, and increased cardiovascular risk. Contraindicated in renal failure, active peptic ulcer disease, and third trimester of pregnancy.

title: Amoxicillin
content: Amoxicillin is a broad-spectrum penicillin-type antibiotic used to treat bacterial infections including respiratory tract infections, urinary tract infections, skin infections, and H. pylori eradication. Typical adult dose is 250mg to 500mg three times daily or 875mg twice daily. It is available in capsule, tablet, and oral suspension forms. Common side effects include diarrhoea, nausea, and skin rashes. Contraindicated in patients with penicillin allergy. Should not be used for viral infections.

title: Metformin
content: Metformin is a biguanide antidiabetic drug and the first-line pharmacological treatment for type 2 diabetes mellitus. It works by reducing hepatic glucose production and improving insulin sensitivity. Starting dose is typically 500mg once or twice daily with meals, titrated up to a maximum of 2000mg to 2500mg per day. Common side effects are GI related: nausea, diarrhoea, and abdominal discomfort. Rare but serious side effect is lactic acidosis. Contraindicated in eGFR less than 30 mL/min, hepatic failure, and use of IV contrast agents.

title: Atorvastatin
content: Atorvastatin is an HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor (statin) used to lower LDL cholesterol and reduce cardiovascular risk. Common doses are 10mg, 20mg, 40mg, and 80mg once daily, usually taken at night. Brand names include Lipitor and Storvas. Side effects include myalgia, elevated liver enzymes, and rarely rhabdomyolysis. Contraindicated in active liver disease, pregnancy, and breastfeeding. Drug interactions with CYP3A4 inhibitors like clarithromycin and grapefruit juice can increase statin levels.

title: Amlodipine
content: Amlodipine is a calcium channel blocker used for hypertension and angina. It causes vasodilation by blocking L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle. Usual dose is 5mg to 10mg once daily. Common side effects include peripheral oedema, flushing, and headache. It is safe in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. Brand names include Norvasc and Amcard. No significant interaction with renal function; it can be used in chronic kidney disease.

title: Omeprazole
content: Omeprazole is a proton pump inhibitor (PPI) that reduces gastric acid secretion by irreversibly inhibiting H+/K+ ATPase in gastric parietal cells. It is used for GERD, peptic ulcer disease, H. pylori eradication (as part of triple therapy), and Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. Typical dose is 20mg to 40mg once daily before meals. Common side effects include headache, diarrhoea, nausea, and abdominal pain. Long-term use is associated with hypomagnesaemia, vitamin B12 deficiency, and increased risk of C. difficile infection.

title: Salbutamol (Albuterol)
content: Salbutamol is a short-acting beta-2 adrenergic agonist (SABA) used as a bronchodilator in asthma and COPD. It relaxes bronchial smooth muscle and provides quick relief of bronchospasm. Inhaler dose is 100mcg to 200mcg (1 to 2 puffs) as needed, up to 4 times daily. Nebulisation dose is 2.5mg every 20 minutes for acute exacerbations. Side effects include tachycardia, tremor, hypokalaemia at high doses, and palpitations. Overuse may indicate poorly controlled asthma requiring step-up therapy.

title: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
content: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) is a chronic metabolic disorder characterised by insulin resistance and relative insulin deficiency. Diagnostic criteria include fasting plasma glucose ≥126 mg/dL, 2-hour glucose ≥200 mg/dL during OGTT, HbA1c ≥6.5%, or random glucose ≥200 mg/dL with symptoms. Management includes lifestyle modification, metformin as first-line therapy, and addition of other agents such as SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 agonists, DPP-4 inhibitors, or insulin as needed. Target HbA1c is generally less than 7% for most patients. Complications include diabetic nephropathy, retinopathy, neuropathy, and cardiovascular disease.

title: Hypertension
content: Hypertension is defined as sustained blood pressure ≥140/90 mmHg (JNC 7) or ≥130/80 mmHg (ACC/AHA 2017 guidelines). It is classified as primary (essential, 90-95% of cases) or secondary. Risk factors include obesity, high salt diet, sedentary lifestyle, family history, and chronic kidney disease. First-line antihypertensive agents include ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, and thiazide diuretics. Target BP is generally less than 130/80 mmHg in high-risk patients. Hypertension is a major risk factor for stroke, myocardial infarction, heart failure, and chronic kidney disease.

title: Asthma
content: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory airway disease characterised by reversible airflow obstruction, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and airway remodelling. Symptoms include wheezing, shortness of breath, chest tightness, and cough, typically worse at night or early morning. Diagnosis is confirmed by spirometry showing FEV1/FVC ratio less than 0.70 with significant bronchodilator reversibility (≥12% and 200mL increase in FEV1). Management follows GINA stepwise approach: SABA for reliever, ICS as controller, with step-up to LABA, LTRA, or biologics for severe asthma. Triggers include allergens, exercise, cold air, smoke, and NSAIDs.

title: Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP)
content: Community-acquired pneumonia is an acute infection of the lung parenchyma acquired outside the hospital setting. Common causative organisms include Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, Mycoplasma pneumoniae, and respiratory viruses. Symptoms include fever, productive cough, dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, and crackles on auscultation. Diagnosis is clinical and confirmed by chest X-ray showing new infiltrate. Severity is assessed using CRB-65 or PSI score. Treatment for mild CAP includes amoxicillin or doxycycline. Severe CAP requires IV beta-lactam plus macrolide or fluoroquinolone monotherapy.

title: Urinary Tract Infection (UTI)
content: Urinary tract infection refers to bacterial infection of the urinary tract, classified as uncomplicated (lower UTI/cystitis in healthy non-pregnant women) or complicated (upper UTI/pyelonephritis, or infection in males, pregnant women, or immunocompromised patients). Common causative organisms include E. coli (80%), Klebsiella, Proteus, and Staphylococcus saprophyticus. Symptoms of cystitis include dysuria, frequency, urgency, and suprapubic discomfort. Pyelonephritis presents with fever, rigors, loin pain, and nausea. Diagnosis is by urine dipstick and culture. Treatment for uncomplicated UTI includes nitrofurantoin, trimethoprim, or fosfomycin. Complicated UTI requires broader spectrum antibiotics.

title: Anaemia
content: Anaemia is defined as haemoglobin below 13 g/dL in adult males and below 12 g/dL in adult females. Common types include iron-deficiency anaemia (most common worldwide), vitamin B12 or folate deficiency (megaloblastic), anaemia of chronic disease, and haemolytic anaemia. Iron-deficiency anaemia presents with fatigue, pallor, koilonychia, and pica. Investigation includes CBC, peripheral smear, serum ferritin, B12, folate, and reticulocyte count. Iron-deficiency anaemia is treated with oral ferrous sulfate 200mg twice to three times daily. B12 deficiency is treated with IM cyanocobalamin or high-dose oral B12.

title: Complete Blood Count (CBC) Reference Ranges
content: Normal CBC reference ranges for adults: Haemoglobin: 13.0–17.5 g/dL (male), 12.0–15.5 g/dL (female). Haematocrit: 41–53% (male), 36–46% (female). RBC count: 4.5–5.9 ×10^12/L (male), 4.0–5.2 ×10^12/L (female). WBC count: 4.0–11.0 ×10^9/L. Platelet count: 150–400 ×10^9/L. MCV: 80–100 fL. MCH: 27–33 pg. MCHC: 32–36 g/dL. Neutrophils: 2.0–7.5 ×10^9/L (40–70%). Lymphocytes: 1.5–4.0 ×10^9/L (20–45%). Monocytes: 0.2–1.0 ×10^9/L (2–10%). Eosinophils: 0.04–0.4 ×10^9/L (1–6%). Basophils: 0–0.1 ×10^9/L (0–1%).

title: Metabolic Panel Reference Ranges
content: Normal metabolic panel reference ranges: Fasting glucose: 70–99 mg/dL (3.9–5.5 mmol/L). HbA1c: below 5.7% normal, 5.7–6.4% prediabetes, ≥6.5% diabetes. Serum creatinine: 0.6–1.2 mg/dL (male), 0.5–1.1 mg/dL (female). BUN (blood urea nitrogen): 7–20 mg/dL. eGFR: ≥90 mL/min/1.73m² normal, 60–89 mild CKD, 30–59 moderate CKD, 15–29 severe CKD, below 15 kidney failure. Sodium: 135–145 mEq/L. Potassium: 3.5–5.0 mEq/L. Chloride: 98–106 mEq/L. Bicarbonate: 22–29 mEq/L. Total calcium: 8.5–10.5 mg/dL. Serum albumin: 3.5–5.0 g/dL.

title: Lipid Panel Reference Ranges
content: Lipid panel reference ranges: Total cholesterol: desirable below 200 mg/dL, borderline 200–239 mg/dL, high ≥240 mg/dL. LDL cholesterol: optimal below 100 mg/dL, near optimal 100–129 mg/dL, borderline 130–159 mg/dL, high 160–189 mg/dL, very high ≥190 mg/dL. HDL cholesterol: low below 40 mg/dL (male), below 50 mg/dL (female); desirable above 60 mg/dL. Triglycerides: normal below 150 mg/dL, borderline 150–199 mg/dL, high 200–499 mg/dL, very high ≥500 mg/dL. In high cardiovascular risk patients, LDL target is below 70 mg/dL.

title: Liver Function Tests (LFT) Reference Ranges
content: Normal liver function test reference ranges: ALT (alanine aminotransferase): 7–56 U/L. AST (aspartate aminotransferase): 10–40 U/L. ALP (alkaline phosphatase): 44–147 U/L. GGT (gamma-glutamyl transferase): 9–48 U/L. Total bilirubin: 0.1–1.2 mg/dL. Direct bilirubin: 0.0–0.3 mg/dL. Indirect bilirubin: 0.2–0.8 mg/dL. Total protein: 6.3–8.2 g/dL. Albumin: 3.5–5.0 g/dL. ALT and AST elevation above 3× ULN is considered clinically significant. PT/INR is used to assess synthetic function of the liver; normal INR is 0.9–1.1.

title: Thyroid Function Tests Reference Ranges
content: Thyroid function test reference ranges: TSH (thyroid stimulating hormone): 0.4–4.0 mIU/L. Free T4 (thyroxine): 0.8–1.8 ng/dL. Free T3 (triiodothyronine): 2.3–4.2 pg/mL. Hypothyroidism: elevated TSH, low free T4. Subclinical hypothyroidism: elevated TSH, normal free T4. Hyperthyroidism: suppressed TSH, elevated free T4 and/or T3. Subclinical hyperthyroidism: suppressed TSH, normal free T4 and T3. TPO antibodies elevated in Hashimoto thyroiditis. TSH receptor antibodies elevated in Graves disease. First-line treatment for hypothyroidism is levothyroxine.

title: Normal Vital Signs — Adults
content: Normal adult vital sign ranges: Heart rate: 60–100 beats per minute. Respiratory rate: 12–20 breaths per minute. Blood pressure: systolic 90–120 mmHg, diastolic 60–80 mmHg. Temperature: 36.1°C to 37.2°C (97°F to 99°F). SpO2 (oxygen saturation): 95–100% on room air. Mean arterial pressure (MAP): 70–100 mmHg. Tachycardia: HR above 100 bpm. Bradycardia: HR below 60 bpm. Tachypnoea: RR above 20 breaths/min. Fever: temperature above 38.0°C (100.4°F). Hypotension: systolic BP below 90 mmHg. Hypertensive crisis: systolic above 180 mmHg or diastolic above 120 mmHg.

title: Paediatric Vital Sign Ranges
content: Normal paediatric vital sign ranges by age group: Newborn (0–1 month): HR 100–160 bpm, RR 30–60/min, SBP 60–90 mmHg. Infant (1–12 months): HR 100–160 bpm, RR 30–60/min, SBP 70–100 mmHg. Toddler (1–3 years): HR 90–150 bpm, RR 24–40/min, SBP 80–110 mmHg. Preschool (3–5 years): HR 80–140 bpm, RR 22–34/min, SBP 80–110 mmHg. School age (6–12 years): HR 70–120 bpm, RR 18–30/min, SBP 85–120 mmHg. Adolescent (12–18 years): HR 60–100 bpm, RR 12–20/min, SBP 95–140 mmHg. Temperature normal range same as adults across all age groups.
